narrative:
As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38.50, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many peers would envy. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt. This high failure risk triggered the rejection of your law school application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers.

The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, which was the strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction—it's notably lower than the average 5.53, signaling a dip in performance that the model sees as a major red flag for bar exam readiness. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which worked strongly in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, reflecting how your above-average result demonstrates the analytical skills needed for the exam. Rounding out the top factors, your Year 1 decile of 6th—slightly above the 5.71 average—still nudged the outcome toward failure, though less forcefully, as it suggests your early promise didn't fully carry through.

Compared to typical students in the dataset, your profile stands out positively in test scores and GPA, but those weaker decile rankings in later years outweighed them, placing you in a higher-risk group for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with your background—many with similar profiles succeed—but the model weighs these academic trends heavily based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level prep that addresses any mid-stream slumps. You could also gather letters from professors highlighting your resilience, or explore post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your transcript before reapplying. With your LSAT and GPA as anchors, targeted improvements here could flip the model's outlook next time. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
